title: Music publishers to proceed with revised Anthropic lawsuit - Music Ally
text:
AI giant Anthropic has been denied its motion to dismiss a lawsuit brought against it by music publishers alleging copyright infringement of their lyrics.
It’s a setback for the company, because previously the publishers’ lawsuit had been narrowed down by Judge Eumi K. Lee. However, they were allowed to refile, and Lee has declined to throw out the amended complaints. The case will now proceed.
CMU has a good summary of the arguments
, noting that publishers claim that ‘guardrails’ put in place by Anthropic to stop its Claude chatbot outputting copyrighted lyrics is key.
“Publishers argue that the court can infer that Anthropic had ‘actual or constructive knowledge’ that Claude users infringed specific lyrics based on Anthropic’s implementation and development of ‘guardrails’,” is how Lee put it in her ruling.
This isn’t yet an established fact: the ruling is merely Lee saying that this claim is “plausible” so can proceed, rather than be dismissed. The test of this (and the publishers’ other claims) will come in a full trial. Or will it?
The last time Anthropic had a setback in a high-profile copyright lawsuit – one filed by book authors –
it quickly agreed a $1.5bn settlement
. It increasingly feels like a settlement will also be the ultimate resolution in the music-publishing case too.
That isn’t just Music Ally going out on a limb.
Today’s Financial Times has a report
on how Anthropic and rival OpenAI are “considering using investor funds to settle potential claims from multibillion-dollar lawsuits” – spurred by the fact that they may struggle to secure enough insurance coverage for the potential damages in these cases.
Anthropic closed a $13bn Series F funding round in September
, just before announcing its book-authors settlement. With the music publishers’ case now proceeding in full, that may provide the impetus to get back to the negotiating table and offer a settlement big enough to be unrefusable.
How big might that be? We sense the answer to that question may come this side of Christmas.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
